Title: Guard cell-specific glycine decarboxylase manipulation affects Arabidopsis photosynthesis, growth and stomatal behavior

Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite: Leaf carbohydrates (sucrose, glucose, fructose, transitory starch); 3-phosphoglycerate
Pathway_Genes.Terminal_Metabolite_Function: Supports photosynthesis, growth, and guard-cell energy supply for stomatal movements
Pathway_Genes.Biosynthetic_Pathway: Photorespiration; linked Calvin-Benson cycle and carbohydrate biosynthesis
Pathway_Genes.Pathway_Branch_or_Subpathway: Mitochondrial glycine decarboxylation step of photorespiration
Pathway_Genes.Protein_Family_or_Domain: H-protein of the glycine decarboxylase/glycine cleavage system; lipoate-bearing carrier protein
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Substrate: Glycine (as substrate of the glycine decarboxylase complex step carried by H-protein)
Pathway_Genes.Primary_Product: Facilitates glycine decarboxylation leading to photorespiratory CO2 release, NH3 production, and serine formation downstream
Pathway_Genes.Catalyzed_Reaction_Description: Functions as the H-protein carrier component of the mitochondrial glycine decarboxylase complex that mediates glycine decarboxylation during photorespiration
Pathway_Genes.End_Product_Connection_Type: controls photorespiratory flux that supports Calvin-Benson cycle performance and promotes carbohydrate accumulation
Pathway_Genes.Rate_Limiting_or_Key_Control_Step: Supported as a key control point of photorespiratory carbon flux
Pathway_Genes.Expression_Pattern_of_Applied_Species: Guard cell-specific transgene expression driven by GC1 promoter; mesophyll and whole-leaf GDC-H remained largely unchanged
Pathway_Genes.Applied_Promoters: Arabidopsis GC1 promoter (guard cell-preferential, inducible by cell type specificity)
Pathway_Genes.Core_Validation_Method: Guard cell-specific overexpression and antisense suppression; immunoblotting of guard-cell vs mesophyll fractions; gas exchange; chlorophyll fluorescence; metabolite profiling by LC-MS/MS and GC; guard-cell starch microscopy
Pathway_Genes.Core_Phenotypic_Effect: Higher guard-cell GDC-H increased stomatal conductance, transpiration, net CO2 assimilation, leaf carbohydrate accumulation, guard-cell starch, and biomass; reduced GDC-H caused the opposite
Pathway_Genes.Environment_or_Treatment_Factor: Ambient air vs elevated CO2 (3000 ppm); varying O2 concentrations (3%, 21%, 40%); different photoperiods
Pathway_Genes.Omics_Data: Metabolomics (LC-MS/MS; GC); no public accession reported
Pathway_Genes.Potential_Tradeoffs: Reduced GDC-H caused lower growth and photosynthesis; overexpression increased transpiration together with higher stomatal conductance
Pathway_Genes.Breeding_or_Engineering_Application_Valu: Guard-cell-targeted enhancement of photorespiration could be used to improve photosynthesis and growth under atmospheres requiring high photorespiratory capacity, with caution regarding transpiration/water use
Pathway_Genes.Summary_Key_Findings_of_Core_Gene: 1. Guard cell-specific manipulation of GDC-H altered GDC-H abundance only in guard cells, not mesophyll. 2. Increased GDC-H improved stomatal conductance, net CO2 assimilation, O2 tolerance of photosynthesis, carbohydrate accumulation, guard-cell starch, and biomass. 3. Antisense suppression reduced these traits. 4. Effects were negligible under high CO2 or low O2 that suppress photorespiration, linking the phenotype to photorespiratory flux in guard cells.
Pathway_Genes.Other_Important_Info: The manipulated coding sequence was from Flaveria pringlei but the metabolic and physiological effects were evaluated in Arabidopsis guard cells and whole plants; H-protein is a carrier component rather than the catalytic P-protein, but the paper treats GDC-H abundance as a proxy/manipulation point for GDC activity and photorespiratory flux.
